turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 16-498, Patchak v.  Zinke, Justice Thomas has the announcement of our judgment.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on the writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit. In 2005, the Secretary of Interior took land into trust for the Gun Lake Tribe of Michigan, so that the tribe could build a casino. A nearby landowner, David Patchak, filed a lawsuit challenging the trust status of the property. While Patchak's suit was pending, Congress enacted the Gun Lake Act. Section 2(a) of the Act ratifies the Secretary's decision to take the property into trust. Section 2(b) stated that the actions relating to the property "shall not be filed or maintained in a federal court and shall be promptly dismissed." Based on Section 2(b) the Secretary moved to dismiss Patchak's suit for lack of jurisdiction. The District Court granted the motion and the DC Circuit affirmed. We now affirm. Section 2(b) of the Gun Lake Act does not violate Section 3 of the Constitution. Justices Breyer, Alito and Kagan have joined an opinion that I have authored. Justice Breyer has also filed a concurring opinion. Justice Ginsburg has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, which Justice Sotomayor has joined. In addition, Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment. The Chief Justice has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justices Kennedy and Gorsuch have joined.
